---
title: "Multi-Class Machine Learning Approaches to Student Dropout"
subtitle: "A Comparative Study of Classifiers and Data Balancing Techniques"
author: "Kareem D. Piper (Advisor: Dr. Shusen Pu)"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Agenda"
    center: true
    incremental: false
    theme: blood
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    include-after-body: 
      - text: |
          <style>
          .reveal { background-color: #000000; }
          .reveal section { color: #ffffff; }
          /* Title slide styling */
          .reveal .title { color: #00FFFF; text-shadow: 0 0 15px #00FFFF; font-size: 1.8em; }
          .reveal .subtitle { color: #FF00FF; text-shadow: 0 0 10px #FF00FF; font-style: italic; }
          .reveal .author, .reveal .date { color: #00FF00; margin-top: 20px; }
          /* General heading styling */
          .reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: #00FFFF; text-shadow: 0 0 10px #00FFFF; }
          .reveal a { color: #FF00FF; }
          .reveal .progress { color: #00FF00; }
          /* Table styling for dark mode */
          table { border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; color: white; border: 1px solid #00FFFF; }
          th { background-color: #1a1a1a; color: #00FFFF; }
          td, th { border: 1px solid #333; padding: 10px; text-align: left; }
          </style>
course: Capstone Projects in Data Science
bibliography: references.bib
self-contained: true
execute:
  cwd: .
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
editor:
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
# Core libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker
from matplotlib.ticker import MaxNLocator

# Scikit-learn utilities
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

# Metrics
from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score,
    precision_score,
    recall_score,
    f1_score,
    classification_report,
    confusion_matrix,
    make_scorer
)

# Models
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from lightgbm import LGBMClassifier
from catboost import CatBoostClassifier

# Imbalance handling
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline as ImbPipeline
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler
from imblearn.over_sampling import RandomOverSampler, SMOTE
from imblearn.combine import SMOTEENN, SMOTETomek


# Cyberpunk Plotting Setup
plt.style.use('dark_background')
neon_cyan = '#00FFFF'
neon_magenta = '#FF00FF'
neon_lime = '#00FF00'
colors = [neon_cyan, neon_magenta, neon_lime]

# Data Loading
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

# HARD-SET your repo root (this is the path that contains index.qmd and the data/ folder)
repo_root = Path("/Users/kareempiper/Projects/IDC6940_DataPraxisAI")
data_dir = repo_root / "data"
train_path = data_dir / "train.csv"
test_path  = data_dir / "test.csv"

print("Repo root:", repo_root)
print("Train exists:", train_path.exists(), train_path)
print("Test exists:", test_path.exists(), test_path)

if not (data_dir.exists() and train_path.exists() and test_path.exists()):
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        f"Expected data files not found.\n"
        f"data_dir: {data_dir} (exists={data_dir.exists()})\n"
        f"train: {train_path} (exists={train_path.exists()})\n"
        f"test:  {test_path} (exists={test_path.exists()})"
    )

train_data = pd.read_csv(train_path)
test_data  = pd.read_csv(test_path)

print("Train shape:", train_data.shape)
print("Test shape:", test_data.shape)

# Strip extra spaces from column names
train_data.columns = train_data.columns.str.strip()
test_data.columns = test_data.columns.str.strip()

# Drop id columns if present
train_data.drop(columns=['id'], inplace=True, errors='ignore')
test_data.drop(columns=['id'], inplace=True, errors='ignore')
```


## Introduction {.smaller}

-  The Problem: Student attrition is more than just a metric; it is a catalyst for societal issues. When a student drops out, they face a less competitive job market, leading to economic deficiencies and reduced healthcare access [@realinho2022].

-  The Scale: While many graduate, 30% of US students dropout—frequently in their first year. It is a "silent crisis" in higher education [@aina2022].

-  The Question: Can we move beyond simple statistics to a holistic lens? We need to understand the relationship between sociological, economic, and psychological factors to intervene effectively.

-  The Methodology: By using Machine Learning, we can converge these high-dimensional factors into a predictive "early warning system" [@raschka2022].


## Research Questions {.smaller}

#### Primary Question (RQ1)

-  Which multi-class machine learning classifier achieves the highest predictive performance (Accuracy, Precision, F1-score) on the UCI Student Dropout Dataset?

#### Sub-Questions

-   RQ1a: How do data balancing techniques (SMOTE, RUS) affect performance across skewed classes?

-   RQ1b: How do Gradient Boosting models compare to traditional ensembles in this high-dimensional space?


## Methods : The Dataset 

-   Source: UCI Irvine Machine Learning Repository [@realinho2022].

-   Dataset Characteristics: Tubular dataset meant for social sciences and classification-based projects [@realinho2022].

-   Shape: $n = 4,424$ cases and $n = 36$ features.

-   Target Classes: Dropout, Enrolled, and Graduate.

-   Feature Types: Real, categorical, and integer-based features representing demographic, financial, and academic domains.


## Methods: Class Imbalance Challenge


```{python}
#| label: plot-target-dist
fig1, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(8,5))
tg_cnts = train_data.Target.value_counts()
tg_cnts_pct = (tg_cnts / tg_cnts.sum()) * 100
ax1.bar(tg_cnts.index, tg_cnts_pct, color=colors, edgecolor='white', linewidth=0.5)
ax1.set_ylabel('Percentage')
ax1.set_title('Target Distribution: Class Imbalance', color=neon_cyan)
for i, pct in enumerate(tg_cnts_pct):
    ax1.text(i, pct + 1, f'{pct:.1f}%', ha='center', va='bottom', color='white', fontweight='bold')
plt.show()
```

-   Graduate: 49.9% | Dropout: 32.1% | Enrolled: 17.9%.

-   Why it matters: Skewed data leads to model bias towards the majority (Graduate) class.


## EDA: Descriptive Statistics

#### Table 1: Descriptive Statistics for Selected Numerical Features

| Statistic          | Age at Enrollment | Admission Grade | Curricular Units 2nd Sem (Grade) | Unemployment Rate | Inflation Rate | GDP     |
|:-------------------|:------------------|:----------------|:---------------------------------|:------------------|:---------------|:--------|
| Count              | 3539.0            | 3539.0          | 3539.0                           | 3539.0            | 3539.0         | 3539.0  |
| Mean               | 23.3              | 126.88          | 11.23                            | 11.5              | 1.23           | 1.76    |
| Std                | 6.13              | 14.53           | 2.94                             | 2.62              | 1.63           | 2.33    |
| Min                | 17.0              | 95.0            | 0.0                              | 7.6               | -0.8           | -4.06   |
| 25%                | 19.0              | 117.8           | 10.33                            | 9.4               | 0.3            | 0.58    |
| 50%                | 21.0              | 126.0           | 12.0                             | 11.1              | 1.4            | 1.74    |
| 75%                | 25.0              | 134.9           | 13.0                             | 13.9              | 2.6            | 3.26    |
| Max                | 70.0              | 190.0           | 18.0                             | 16.2              | 3.7            | 3.51    |

- **Observation:** High variance in Age and Admission grades suggests a diverse student population with varying levels of academic readiness.

---

## EDA: Feature Cardinality

- **Why:** Identifying low-cardinality features allows for efficient **One-Hot Encoding** in the pipeline [@namoun2020].

## EDA: Unique Value Table

**Table 2: Unique Value Counts (Binary & Categorical)**

| Feature | Unique Count | Feature Type |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Daytime/evening attendance | 2 | Binary |
| Debtor | 2 | Binary |
| Scholarship holder | 2 | Binary |
| Gender | 2 | Binary |
| Marital status | 6 | Categorical |
| Application order | 8 | Categorical |
---

##  EDA: Correlation Analysis

- **Spearman Correlation Matrix:** Conducted to identify non-linear relationships between numeric features.
- **Key Link:** Strong positive correlation observed between 1st and 2nd-semester curricular units (Approved and Grade).
- **Red Flag:** Categorical features encoded as integers require careful interpretation to avoid false correlation signals [@raschka2022].

## EDA: Spearman Rank Correlation Plot

```{python}
#Select numeric features only (exclude Target)
numeric_cols = train_data.select_dtypes(include=['int64', 'float64']).columns
numeric_cols = [col for col in numeric_cols if col != 'Target']

plt.figure(figsize=(20, 15))
sns.heatmap(
    train_data[numeric_cols].corr(method='spearman'),
    cmap='magma',
    annot=True,
    fmt='.1f',
    linewidths=0.5
)
plt.title('Spearman Correlation Matrix (Numeric Features)')
plt.show()
```

##  EDA: Categorical Distributions

- **What:** Visualized via bar charts to identify the frequency of specific student attributes.
- **Key Finding:** Features like "Displaced" and "Tuition fees up to date" show significant splits across the target classes.
- **Why:** These distributions inform the model's ability to distinguish between a student who is likely to graduate versus one at risk of dropping out.

## EDA: Catagorical Distributions Plot

```{python}
### Identification of Categorical Features

# Identify categorical columns based on low cardinality
cat_cols = [
    col for col in train_data.columns
    if col != 'Target' and train_data[col].nunique() <= 8
]

# Convert to categorical dtype (for CatBoost compatibility later)
for col in cat_cols:
    train_data[col] = train_data[col].astype('category')
    test_data[col] = test_data[col].astype('category')

print("Categorical columns:")
print(cat_cols)
```

```{python}
# Categorical feature distributions
n_cols = 4
n_rows = int(np.ceil(len(cat_cols) / n_cols))

fig, axs = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(11, 2.2 * n_rows))
axs = np.array(axs).ravel()

# Define a cyberpunk color palette
cyberpunk_palette = sns.color_palette('magma', n_colors=8)

for ax, col in zip(axs, cat_cols):
    vc = train_data[col].value_counts(normalize=True).sort_index()
    # Use the color palette for bars, cycling through it if more categories than colors
    ax.bar(vc.index.astype(str), vc, color=cyberpunk_palette[:len(vc)])
    ax.set_title(col, fontsize=10)
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x:.0%}'))
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45)

# Turn off any unused axes
for ax in axs[len(cat_cols):]:
    ax.set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

```


## Methods: Data Preparation and Preprocessing

-   The following steps and strategies were implemented to prepare the UCI Student Dropout dataset for analysis, ensuring model robustness and preventing data leakage.

-   Further, I established a structured machine learning pipeline using `ColumnTransformer` to ensure reproducibility and prevent data leakage [@raschka2022].

## Preprocessing Table 1

**Table 3: Preprocessing Workflow (The What & Why)**

| Step | Technique | Purpose (The Why) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Separation** | Feature Matrix ($X$) vs Target ($Y$) | Isolates predictors from the objective. |
| **Casting** | Target as String type | Prevents ordinal interpretation; ensures consistent processing. |
| **Label Encoding** | `LabelEncoder` | Transforms "Dropout", "Enrolled", "Graduate" into (0, 1, 2) [@raschka2022]. |
| **Partitioning** | 80/20 Stratified Split | Ensures original class distribution is preserved in training/validation [@pek2023]. |

## Preprocessing Table 2

**Table 4: Preprocessing Workflow (The What & Why)**

| Feature Type | Strategy | Reason (The Why) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Numerical** | `SimpleImputer` (Median) | Addresses potential missing values using the median technique as a precaution and sanity check without skewing distribution [@andradegiron2023]. |
| **Categorical** | `OneHotEncoder` | Converts nominal variables into binary classification to ensure capability with classification algorithms [@namoun2020]. |
| **Cardinality** | Threshold $\le 8$ | Variables with low cardinality are identified for one-hot encoding to ensure nominal variables are addressed [@andradegiron2023]. |

- **Why:** Embedding preprocessing into the pipeline ensures that all transformations are learnt from the training data, enhancing the potential for generalization and reducing data leakage [@raschka2022].


## Methods: Class Imbalance Techniques and Machine Learning Classifiers

## Class Imbalancing Technqies Table

**Table 5: Class Imbalance Techniques (The What & Why)**

| Technique    | Category      | Purpose (The Why)                                                                   |
|:-------------|:--------------|:------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| RUS          | Under-sampling| Reduces majority class instances to match the minority class.                       |
| ROS          | Over-sampling | Replicates minority class instances to balance the distribution.                    |
| SMOTE        | Synthetic     | Creates new synthetic minority examples to increase minority representation.        |
| SMOTE-ENN    | Hybrid        | Over-samples the minority class then cleans noisy data points using Edited Nearest Neighbors. |
| SMOTE-Tomek  | Hybrid        | Removes overlapping class boundaries by identifying and removing Tomek links.       |


## Machine Learning Classfieres Table

**Table 6: Models Used in the Study**

| Model        | Category           |
|:-------------|:-------------------|
| RandomForest | Ensemble (Bagging) |
| XGBoost      | Gradient Boosting  |
| LightGBM     | Gradient Boosting  |
| CatBoost     | Gradient Boosting  |


## Analysis and Results




## Conclusions
